source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240826_Why didn't Jesus say I AM GOD in those Exact Words？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun argues that Jesus did claim divine identity but through Jewish idiom rather than the exact phrase 'I am God'. He cites John 8:58 ('Before Abraham was, I am'), John 10:30 ('I and the Father are one'), and the reaction of Jewish authorities who sought to stone him for blasphemy as implicit claims to divinity. The argument addresses the Muslim objection that Jesus never explicitly claimed to be God.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240831_Was Rebecca 3 In the Bible？ ｜ Genesis 25_20 ｜ Refuting Muslim Objection Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the Muslim claim that Genesis 25:20 implies Rebecca was only 3 years old when she married Isaac. He argues this reading misapplies the genealogical timeline and that the text gives no indication of Rebecca's age.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240902_Does Jesus say to Slay Unbelievers in Luke 19_27？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun argues that Luke 19:27 — 'bring them here and slay them before me' — is not a command to kill unbelievers but the conclusion of the Parable of the Ten Minas (Luke 19:11-27). The nobleman represents Jesus at his Second Coming and the slaying refers to eschatological judgment, not a present command to violence. The verse is frequently cited by Muslims as evidence Jesus commanded killing, but removing it from its parabolic context inverts its meaning.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240907_Did God or Satan Harden Pharaohs Heart & Provoke David to number？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses two apparent contradictions: Exodus 4:21 says God hardened Pharaoh's heart while Exodus 8:15 says Pharaoh hardened his own heart; and 2 Samuel 24:1 says God provoked David to number Israel while 1 Chronicles 21:1 attributes this to Satan. He argues both represent divine sovereignty working through secondary causes.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240912_When did Disciples see Jesus coming with his Kingdom？ Matthew 16_28 Luke 9_27 Mark 9_1 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the claim that Matthew 16:28 constitutes a false prophecy since the disciples died without seeing the Second Coming. He argues the verse refers to the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-9) which follows immediately, not the Second Coming.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240913_Did Jesus Give the Same Glory to the Apostles？ John 17_22 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 17:22 — 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them' — responding to the claim this proves Jesus is not uniquely divine since he shares his glory with the disciples. He argues the sharing of glory is participatory and relational, not ontological.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240915_Did God Dash Babies in Psalm 137_9 ？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Psalm 137:9 — 'Blessed is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks' — cited as evidence of biblical violence. He argues this is a lament psalm expressing the raw emotional anguish of Jewish exiles in Babylon, not a divine command. The verse reflects a human desire for retributive justice, not God's prescription for conduct.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240918_Does God say rebellious children be put to DEATH？ Leviticus 20_9 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Leviticus 20:9 — 'Anyone who curses their father or mother is to be put to death' — cited as evidence of biblical barbarism. He argues this was a civil law for ancient Israel functioning as a theocracy, not a universal moral command for all times, and that Christians are not under the Mosaic civil code.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240920_Hidden Meaning BEHIND Jesus Name & Immanuel Matthew 1_23.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun examines Matthew 1:23's quotation of Isaiah 7:14 — 'the virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel.' He addresses the translation controversy around the Hebrew word 'almah' (young woman vs. virgin), arguing the Septuagint's 'parthenos' (virgin) reflects the correct interpretation, with Matthew's explicit application to Jesus as definitive. The verse is a key point of contention between Christian and Jewish/Muslim interpretations.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240924_Did Paul say Women Remain Silent in a Church？ 1 Corinthians 14_35 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 — 'women should remain silent in the churches' — examining whether this is a universal prohibition. He argues the passage refers to interrupting prophecy with questions in the Corinthian context, not a blanket prohibition, pointing to 1 Corinthians 11 where Paul assumes women do pray and prophesy publicly.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240925_Was John the baptist Elijah to come Malachi 4_1 Matthew 11_14 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the apparent contradiction between Matthew 11:14 where Jesus says John the Baptist 'is the Elijah who was to come' and John 1:21 where John denies being Elijah. He argues John fulfilled the role of Elijah 'in spirit and power' (Luke 1:17) without being a literal reincarnation, fulfilling Malachi 4:5-6.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20240927_Did Moses instruct to take little girls in Numbers 31_19 & Deuteronomy 21_11 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Numbers 31:17-18 — Moses instructing to keep alive young girls who have not known a man — cited as parallel to Muhammad's treatment of captives. He argues the passage concerns incorporation of female captives as protected persons under Mosaic law, not sexual exploitation, and that Deuteronomy 21:10-14 provided unprecedented protections in the ancient Near Eastern context.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241009_Why did Jesus say "My God Why have you Forsaken me？ " Matthew 27_46 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Matthew 27:46 — Jesus crying 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — cited as evidence that Jesus is not God since God cannot forsake himself. He argues this is a deliberate quotation of Psalm 22:1 whose Jewish audience would recognize as ending in vindication and resurrection, and addresses the theological concept of the Son experiencing human abandonment without ontological separation from the Father.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241010_Why did Jesus say "You are gods" John 10_34 (SECRET MEANING) ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 10:34 — 'Is it not written in your Law, I have said you are gods?' (quoting Psalm 82:6) — cited to argue Jesus was claiming only a lower form of divinity available to humans. He argues Jesus uses the verse a fortiori: if human judges could be called 'gods' functionally, how much more appropriate for the eternal Son whom the Father sent into the world.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241011_Why did Jesus have to leave to RECEIVE Holy Spirit ？ John 16_7 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 16:7 — 'Unless I go away, the Advocate will not come to you' — and the Muslim claim this prophesies Muhammad as the Paraclete. He argues the Greek 'Paraclete' cannot refer to Muhammad since Jesus says the Spirit 'will be in you' (John 14:17) and will be sent specifically to the disciples who already knew Jesus.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241013_What did Paul mean by " I BECAME a jew to WIN OVER the jews" 1 Corinthians 9_20 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses 1 Corinthians 9:20 — 'To the Jews I became like a Jew' — sometimes cited as evidence Paul was deceptive or inconsistent. He argues Paul describes missiological cultural accommodation: adapting practices (not doctrines) to reach different audiences, as illustrated in Acts 16:3 (circumcising Timothy) and Acts 21.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241014_Can the Disciples forgive Sins Like Jesus？ John 20_23 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 20:23 — 'If you forgive anyone's sins, their sins are forgiven' — examining what authority the disciples received and whether this supports Catholic sacramental confession. He argues the disciples pronounced forgiveness as ambassadors declaring God's forgiveness on the basis of faith, not as independent sources of forgiveness.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241015_Why did Jesus curse a Fig Tree？ (DEEP MEANING) Mark  11_14 Matthew 21_19 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the cursing of the fig tree in Mark 11:14, noting the apparent irrationality of cursing a tree for not having fruit out of season. He argues this is a prophetic enacted parable — the fig tree represents Israel with outward religious appearance but no spiritual fruit, prophetically anticipating the judgment on Jerusalem in 70 AD.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241017_Jesus "This Generation Shall Not Pass Away" False Prophecy？ Matthew 24_34 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Matthew 24:34 — 'this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have happened' — cited as a failed prophecy since the Second Coming did not occur within Jesus's generation. He argues 'this generation' refers either to a future generation that sees the described signs, or that the verse refers specifically to the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD which did occur within that generation.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241019_Do the Dead perish and know nothing？ Ecclesiastes 9_5.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Ecclesiastes 9:5 — 'the dead know nothing' — cited by Jehovah's Witnesses for soul sleep or annihilationism. He argues Ecclesiastes reflects earthly wisdom 'under the sun' not divine revelation about the afterlife, and must be read alongside Luke 16:19-31 and Revelation 6:9-11 which indicate conscious existence after death.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241101_Does Jesus have 2 DIFFERENT Genealogies Contradiction in the Bible ？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the apparent contradiction between the genealogy of Jesus in Matthew 1:1-17 (through Solomon) and Luke 3:23-38 (through Nathan), both tracing lineage to David through different sons. He argues Matthew traces Joseph's legal lineage while Luke traces Mary's biological lineage, or alternatively that one represents a levirate marriage succession.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241111_Was Jesus Sent ONLY to lost Sheep of Israel？ Matthew 15_24  ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Matthew 15:24 — 'I was sent only to the lost sheep of Israel' — cited to argue Jesus's mission was exclusively Jewish, making Muhammad's universal mission superior. He argues this refers to Jesus's earthly ministry focus, not the scope of his salvation, pointing to the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19 and Jesus's healing of Gentiles throughout his ministry.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241112_Why Jesus Calls FATHER the ONE True God ｜ John 17_3 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 17:3 — 'that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent' — cited as evidence Jesus distinguished the Father as the only true God and himself as a separate lesser being. He argues this reflects the functional subordination of the Son's incarnate role, not an ontological statement, and that the Son's divine identity is affirmed throughout John's Gospel.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241119_Was Thomas Rebuked by Jesus for Calling Him God？ ｜ John 20_28 ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun examines John 20:28 — Thomas's declaration 'My Lord and my God!' — as one of the clearest explicit identifications of Jesus as God in the New Testament. He addresses the Muslim claim that Jesus rebuked Thomas for this statement, pointing out that Jesus accepted and commended Thomas's faith without any correction.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241203_Why Jesus say DONT DO VAIN REPETITION？ ｜Matthew 6_7  ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Matthew 6:7 — 'when you pray, do not keep on babbling like pagans' — used by Protestants against Catholic/Orthodox repetitive prayer. He argues the prohibition is against mindless repetition without meaning, not structured or persistent prayer, pointing to Jesus praying the same words three times in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:44) and the persistent widow parable.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241218_Why Jesus said Your God and My God in John 20_17 ？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses John 20:17 — 'I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God' — cited as evidence Jesus acknowledged God as his superior. He argues this reflects Jesus speaking in his incarnate human nature post-resurrection, and that 'my Father and your Father' highlights the distinction between Jesus's unique divine sonship and the disciples' adopted sonship.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20241224_Does God say he is NOT a MAN or a SON of MAN in the Bible ？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Numbers 23:19 — 'God is not human, that he should lie, not a human being, that he should change his mind' — cited to argue the incarnation is impossible or contradictory. He argues this describes God's moral and ontological perfection in contrast to human fallibility, not an absolute prohibition on taking human form, and that the incarnation does not compromise divine attributes.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250106_Muslim Asks Sam Shamoun Zakir Naiks Jesus Did Fulfill the Sign Of Jonah ？.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun responds to Zakir Naik's argument that Jesus did not fulfill the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:40) because Jonah was alive in the fish while Jesus died. He argues the sign concerns the duration and miraculous nature of the event, not the exact circumstances, and addresses Naik's claim that the Friday-to-Sunday timeline cannot accommodate three full days and nights.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250112_Why is JESUS called FIRST BORN of all CREATION？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Colossians 1:15 — 'the firstborn over all creation' — cited by Jehovah's Witnesses and Muslims as evidence Jesus was the first created being. He argues 'firstborn' (prototokos) in its Jewish context means preeminence and sovereignty over creation, not chronological first, pointing to Psalm 89:27 where David is called 'firstborn' despite being the youngest son.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250128_Why did God WRESTLE with Jacob and LOSE？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Genesis 32:22-32 — God wrestling with Jacob — examining who Jacob wrestled with and why God appeared to lose. He argues this is a Christophany (pre-incarnate appearance of the Son), that God 'lost' deliberately to test Jacob's persistence, and connects Jacob's statement 'I have seen God face to face' to the broader theme of divine appearances in the Hebrew Bible.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250210_Did Jesus Say I am God or Worship Me？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the Muslim objection 'Where did Jesus explicitly say I am God, worship me?' He argues Jesus demonstrated divine identity through actions (forgiving sins, accepting worship, claiming authority over the Sabbath), titles ('I AM', 'Son of Man' from Daniel 7), and the reactions of contemporaries who sought to stone him for blasphemy — together constituting an unmistakable claim to divinity.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250214_What Did Jesus MEAN by Fulfill not ABOLISH the Law？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Matthew 5:17 — 'I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them' — examining what 'fulfill' means and whether Christians must keep the Mosaic law. He argues Jesus fulfilled the law by embodying its intent perfectly and bringing it to its intended goal, not merely by obeying it.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250228_Is Melchizedek JESUS in the Old Testament？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun examines Hebrews 7's use of Psalm 110:4 — 'You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek' — arguing Melchizedek (Genesis 14:18-20) prefigures or may be a Christophany of Jesus. He addresses the mysterious description 'without father or mother, without genealogy, without beginning of days or end of life' as pointing to the eternal priesthood of Christ.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250417_Sam Shamoun REFUTES Zakir Naik on Did God make Pregnancy a Curse？.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses Zakir Naik's claim that Genesis 3:16 — 'I will make your pains in childbearing very severe' — shows the Bible views pregnancy as a divine curse. He argues the passage describes consequences of the Fall affecting childbirth, not a condemnation of pregnancy itself, and that the broader biblical narrative affirms children as a blessing.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250505_Does the Bible in Deuteronomy Predict Muhammad？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the Muslim claim that Deuteronomy 18:18 — 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites' — prophesies Muhammad. He argues the text requires the prophet to be from the Israelites (not the Ishmaelite line), that Acts 3:22 applies this verse to Jesus, and that Jesus fits the criteria of being 'like Moses' in ways Muhammad does not.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20250915_Sam Shamoun TROUBLES Muslim READING Isaiah 53 is about Jesus.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the Muslim and Jewish counter-claim that Isaiah 53 refers to the nation of Israel rather than a messianic individual. He argues the text describes a single individual whose suffering is vicarious ('he was pierced for our transgressions'), that Israel cannot be the servant suffering for Israel's sins, and that ancient Jewish sources before Christianity also read this passage as referring to the Messiah.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20260106_Why was 1 John 5_7 REMOVED in some Bibles？ ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the Comma Johanneum — 1 John 5:7 in the KJV ('For there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost') — absent from most modern Bible translations. He examines the manuscript evidence, the late appearance of this text, and whether its removal undermines the doctrine of the Trinity.

source_file: Shamounian Explains/20260104_What "Eye for an Eye" Actually Means in the Bible ｜ Sam Shamoun.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamoun addresses the lex talionis principle — 'eye for an eye' (Exodus 21:24, Leviticus 24:20) — often cited as evidence of biblical harshness or compared unfavourably to the Quran. He argues this was a principle of proportional justice limiting revenge (not mandating it), revolutionary in its ancient context, and that Jesus's 'turn the other cheek' (Matthew 5:38) addresses personal retaliation not judicial systems.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20140101_Debate： Dr Zakir Naik v⧸s Pastor Ruknuddin Pio： Was Christ (pbuh) Really Crucified？ Part-1.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik argues the crucifixion of Jesus is not supported by the Gospels themselves and cites the Quran (An-Nisa 4:157) which states Jesus was not crucified but it appeared so to them. He draws on the Swoon Theory and argues the disciples did not witness the crucifixion directly.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250430_Who is the Comforter Who Came after Jesus (pbuh)？ - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik argues that the Paraclete (Comforter/Helper) promised by Jesus in John 14:16 and John 16:7 refers to Prophet Muhammad. He claims the original word was "Periqlytos" (praised one, i.e. Muhammad) which was changed to "Paraclete" (Comforter). He also argues the description — will not speak of himself, will guide into all truth, will be with believers forever — better fits Muhammad than the Holy Spirit.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250514_Why is God Called Father in the Bible but not in the Quran？ - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik addresses why the Bible uses the term Father for God while the Quran avoids it, arguing the Islamic concept of Allah has no human analogies or familial relationships. He argues calling God Father has led to the misconception of a literal father-son relationship in Christianity, which Islam corrects by describing God through his 99 names and attributes.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250827_Meaning of "I am the way and the Truth No Man Comes unto my Father but through me" John ： 146.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik addresses John 14:6 — "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" — arguing this does not prove Jesus is God but rather that Jesus is a guide to God. He argues all prophets could say the same thing — that following them is the way to God for their time and people — and that this verse is consistent with the Islamic view of Jesus as a prophet.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250915_When Jesus Christ (pbuh) Performed Miracles, Whose Power did He Use？ - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik argues Jesus performed miracles by the power of God, not by his own divine power, citing John 5:30 ("I can do nothing on my own") and Acts 2:22 ("Jesus of Nazareth, a man accredited by God to you by miracles, wonders and signs, which God did through him"). He argues this proves Jesus is a prophet performing miracles by divine permission, not God incarnate.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250922_How can the Bible be the Word of God when it Contains Many Contradictions？ - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik argues the Bible cannot be entirely the word of God because it contains contradictions, human errors, and was compiled and edited by human councils. He contrasts this with the Quran which has been preserved unchanged in its original Arabic. He cites examples of numerical contradictions between parallel accounts in Samuel/Kings and Chronicles.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20250924_Can Allah Become a Human Being in Flesh？ - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik addresses the Christian doctrine of the incarnation, arguing it is impossible for the infinite God to become finite. He cites Numbers 23:19 ("God is not a man") and Hosea 11:9 ("I am God, and not a man") as biblical evidence against the incarnation, and argues the Quran explicitly states nothing is like Allah (Surah 42:11).

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20251016_Meaning of the Quranic Verse (2_62) - Dr Zakir Naik.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik addresses Quran 2:62 — "Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews or Christians or Sabeans — those who believed in Allah and the Last Day and did righteousness — will have their reward" — which some interpret as saying non-Muslims can be saved. He argues this verse refers to those who followed their respective prophets faithfully before the coming of Muhammad, not to non-Muslims after Muhammad.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20140222_Muhammad (pbuh) in the Various World Religious Scriptures ｜ Dr Zakir Naik ｜ Part 1.en.vtt
argument_summary: Naik argues Muhammad is prophesied in the Bible, citing Deuteronomy 18:18 ("a prophet like you from among their brothers"), Song of Solomon 5:16 ("his mouth is most sweet, yes he is Muhammad" — claiming the Hebrew "Mahamaddim" is Muhammad's name), and John 16:7 (the Paraclete prophecy). He argues these prophecies cannot refer to Jesus or any other figure.

source_file: Dr Zakir Naik/20131225_The Qur an and the Bible in the Light of Science ｜ Dr Zakir Naik v⧸s Dr William Campbell ｜ Part-3.en.vtt
argument_summary: In debate with Dr William Campbell, Naik argues the Quran contains accurate scientific information about embryology, cosmology, and geology that could not have been known in the 7th century, proving its divine origin. He also argues the Bible contains scientific errors, contrasting the two books. He cites Quran 23:12-14 on embryonic development and Genesis 1 on the order of creation.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20201029_Biblical Proof that Jesus Wasn t Crucified..en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab argues the crucifixion narrative has internal contradictions in the Gospels themselves, citing Mark 15:25 (crucified at the third hour) vs John 19:14 (still before Pilate at the sixth hour). He argues these contradictions undermine the crucifixion account and are consistent with the Quranic position (An-Nisa 4:157) that it only appeared to them that Jesus was crucified.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20210804_Did Paul Distort the Message of Christ？.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab argues Paul introduced theological innovations not taught by Jesus — specifically the doctrine of atonement, the divine status of Jesus, and the abrogation of the Mosaic law. He cites the conflict between Paul and the Jerusalem church (Galatians 2:11-14) as evidence of early Christian disagreement and argues Paul's theology diverged from the original message of Jesus.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20210315_Were Christian Doctrines Plagiarised From Pagans？.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab examines the claim that Christian doctrines like the Trinity, virgin birth, and resurrection were borrowed from pagan mystery religions (Osiris, Mithras, Dionysus). He argues these parallels, while sometimes overstated, demonstrate that the doctrine of God incarnate was a common motif in the ancient world and was adopted by early Christians under Hellenistic influence rather than being original to Jesus.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20190305_MUHAMMAD ﷺ AND MADINAH IN THE BIBLE.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab argues Muhammad is prophesied in the Bible, focusing on Isaiah 42:1-11 which references "Kedar" (an Arabian tribe descended from Ishmael) and a "new song" sung from Sela (identified with Madinah). He argues this passage points specifically to Arabia and Muhammad, not to Jesus or Israel, and that the servant described matches the Prophet's mission.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20240920_HD｜ Is the Trinity Coherent？ ｜ Mohammed Hijab vs Dr. William Lane Craig.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab debates William Lane Craig on the internal coherence of the Trinity doctrine. He argues the classical formulation — one God in three persons, each fully God — is logically contradictory (the problem of numerical identity), and that attempts to resolve it via relative identity or social trinitarianism introduce either modalism or tritheism. He also cites Deuteronomy 6:4 ("Hear O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is One") as evidence of strict monotheism incompatible with Trinitarian theology.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20211106_Mohammed Hijab interviews Dr. Bart Ehrman on Jesus Christ and Biblical Corruption.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab interviews New Testament scholar Bart Ehrman on manuscript corruption in the Bible. Ehrman confirms that key Trinitarian texts — including 1 John 5:7 (the Comma Johanneum) and John 8:1-11 (the woman caught in adultery) — are later additions not found in the earliest manuscripts. Hijab uses this to argue the Bible has been altered and cannot be fully trusted as revelation.

source_file: Mohammed Hijab/20260421_Mohammed Hijab vs GodLogic- FULL DEBATE.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hijab debates GodLogic (Christian apologist) on the nature of Jesus. Key exchanges cover John 1:1 ("the Word was God"), the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4), and whether the New Testament supports Trinitarian or Unitarian monotheism. Hijab argues Jesus consistently presented himself as subordinate to the Father and that Trinitarian theology is a later theological development.

source_file: DUS Dawah/20230625_Shamsi Speaks With A Hebrew Israelite & Discusses Isaiah 42 With A Visitor.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamsi discusses Isaiah 42 with a visitor, arguing the passage prophecies Muhammad rather than Jesus or Israel. He points to verse 42:11 referencing Kedar (Arabian tribes) and the "new song" as indicating an Arabian prophet, and addresses the Christian counter-reading that the servant in Isaiah 42 is Jesus.

source_file: DUS Dawah/20230827_Does The Bible Teach To Kill Babies？ Who Is Really Traversing The Path Of ISIS ？？？.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamsi addresses Psalm 137:9 ("Blessed is the one who dashes your infants against the rocks") and 1 Samuel 15:3 ("kill men and women, children and infants") as examples of commanded violence in the Bible, arguing Christians who criticize Islamic law should address these passages first. He uses these verses in a comparative argument about violence in scripture.

source_file: DUS Dawah/20231105_Is It Okay To Kill Babies & Children？ Christian Flees From His Own Bible!!! ｜ Speakers Corner.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamsi challenges a Christian preacher at Speakers Corner with 1 Samuel 15:3 — God commanding Saul to kill "men and women, children and infants" among the Amalekites — arguing this constitutes divinely commanded genocide. He uses this to challenge the claim that the Biblical God is more merciful than the Quranic Allah.

source_file: DUS Dawah/20240421_Does The Old Testament Agree With The New Testament？ ｜ Speakers Corner Shamsi.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamsi argues the Old and New Testaments contradict each other on fundamental issues including the law (Matthew 5:17 vs Galatians 3:13), the nature of God, and ethical commands. He argues this internal inconsistency shows the Bible has been corrupted or that Christianity represents a departure from the original monotheistic message of the Hebrew prophets.

source_file: DUS Dawah/20250518_Is Prophet Mohamed ﷺ In The Bible？ ｜ Shamsi Speaks To Romanian Christians.en.vtt
argument_summary: Shamsi argues Muhammad is prophesied in the Bible citing Deuteronomy 18:18 (prophet like Moses from among their brothers), John 16:7 (the Paraclete/Comforter), and Song of Solomon 5:16 where the Hebrew word "Mahamaddim" contains the name Muhammad. He addresses a Romanian Christian's objections to each of these arguments.

source_file: Apologetics Roadshow/crucifixion_historical_evidence.en.vtt
argument_summary: The Apologetics Roadshow presents historical evidence for the crucifixion of Jesus from non-Christian sources — Tacitus (Annals 15:44), Josephus (Antiquities 18.3.3), and the Talmud — arguing the crucifixion is one of the most historically attested facts of ancient history. This directly counters the Islamic position based on Quran 4:157 that Jesus was not crucified.

source_file: Apologetics Roadshow/isaiah_53_messiah.en.vtt
argument_summary: The Apologetics Roadshow argues Isaiah 52:13-53:12 is a prophecy about Jesus the Messiah, not the nation of Israel. Key arguments: the servant suffers for the sins of others (vicarious atonement); the servant is distinguished from Israel ("he bore the sin of many"); ancient Jewish sources including the Targum Jonathan identify this as a messianic passage.

source_file: Apologetics Roadshow/trinity_old_testament.en.vtt
argument_summary: The Apologetics Roadshow argues the Trinity is foreshadowed in the Old Testament through plural references to God (Genesis 1:26 "let us make man"), the Angel of the Lord who is identified as God (Genesis 16:13, Exodus 3:2-6), and Proverbs 8 (Wisdom personified). They argue these texts prepared Israel for the fuller New Testament revelation of the triune God.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/john_1_1_word_was_god.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic examines John 1:1 — "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God" — against the Jehovah's Witness rendering "the Word was a god" and the Muslim argument that logos means a created being. He argues the Greek construction (anarthrous predicate nominative) indicates quality of divine nature, not indefinite lesser divinity, and that the entire prologue of John establishes the pre-existent divine identity of Jesus.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/quran_4_157_crucifixion_denial.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic addresses Quran 4:157 — "they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him; but it was made to appear to them so" — arguing this verse contradicts overwhelming historical evidence and even internal Quranic evidence (Surah 19:33 where Jesus says "peace on me the day I die"). He presents the crucifixion as the most historically attested event of Jesus's life.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/deuteronomy_18_18_jesus_not_muhammad.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic responds to the Muslim claim that Deuteronomy 18:18 prophecies Muhammad. He argues the prophet must be "from among their brothers" meaning fellow Israelites (not Ishmaelites), must be like Moses (a lawgiver and mediator), and that Acts 3:22 and Acts 7:37 explicitly apply this verse to Jesus. He addresses the Muslim argument that "brothers" refers to Arab cousins descended from Ishmael.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/jesus_god_attributes_quran.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic argues that the Quran itself attributes divine characteristics to Jesus that it attributes to no other prophet — Jesus is called the Word of Allah (Quran 4:171), a Spirit from Him, born of a virgin, sinless, and able to give life (Surah 3:49). He argues these attributes are inconsistent with Jesus being merely a prophet and point to his unique divine nature.

source_file: Hatun Tash DCCI Ministries/quran_4_157_textual_issues.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hatun Tash examines the textual and theological problems with Quran 4:157 — the denial of the crucifixion. She argues this verse contradicts all non-Muslim historical sources from the 1st and 2nd centuries, places the Quran in direct conflict with Roman records, and that the "it appeared to them" phrasing implies God deceived observers — a theological problem Muslims have not adequately resolved.

source_file: Hatun Tash DCCI Ministries/muhammad_in_bible_refutation.en.vtt
argument_summary: Hatun Tash addresses Muslim claims that Muhammad is prophesied in the Bible (Deuteronomy 18:18, John 16:7, Song of Solomon 5:16). She argues each claimed prophecy fails: Deuteronomy specifies an Israelite prophet; the Paraclete in John is identified as the Holy Spirit (John 14:26) and will be "with you forever" — impossible for Muhammad; and "Mahamaddim" in Song of Solomon is an adjective meaning "lovely things", not a proper name.

source_file: Ali Dawah/jesus_commanded_follow_moses_law.en.vtt
argument_summary: Ali Dawah argues Jesus himself kept and commanded the Mosaic law — citing Matthew 5:17-19, Matthew 23:2-3 ("The teachers of the law and the Pharisees sit in Moses seat; so you must obey them"), and Mark 1:44 (Jesus telling the healed leper to offer the Mosaic sacrifice). He argues this shows the original message of Jesus was Islamic in character — submission to God's law — and that Paul's abrogation of the law was a later corruption.

source_file: Ali Dawah/mark_10_18_jesus_not_good.en.vtt
argument_summary: Ali Dawah highlights Mark 10:18 — "Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone" — as evidence that Jesus denied divine status and distinguished himself from God. He argues if Jesus were God he would not have deflected the title "good" back to God alone, and that this verse directly undermines the Christian claim that Jesus is divine.

source_file: Ali Dawah/john_20_17_jesus_has_a_god.en.vtt
argument_summary: Ali Dawah emphasises John 20:17 — "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God" — as post-resurrection evidence that Jesus is not God. He argues if Jesus were God he could not have a God, and that the resurrected Jesus still explicitly identified the Father as his God, which is incompatible with the Trinitarian claim that Jesus is co-equal with the Father.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20230302_Muslim Reads Isaiah 53 For The First Time!.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic reads Isaiah 53 with a Muslim who has not encountered the passage before, walking through the text verse by verse. The Muslim is confronted with the description of a suffering servant pierced for transgressions, whose soul is made an offering for guilt, and acknowledges it does not fit the nation of Israel. GodLogic argues the passage is a clear prophecy of Jesus that predates Christianity by centuries.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20231101_Muslim Apologist Gets STEAMROLLED on Isaiah 53 ｜ Full Debate.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic debates a Muslim apologist on whether Isaiah 53 refers to Israel or Jesus. He refutes the corporate servant interpretation by showing the servant suffers for Israel ("for the transgression of my people", Isaiah 53:8) meaning the servant cannot be Israel itself, and that the servant's death and burial (53:9) and post-death vindication (53:10-12) describe an individual person.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20231108_Answering Jehovah Witnesses： Is Jesus God Or "a" God？.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic addresses the Jehovah's Witness New World Translation rendering of John 1:1 as "the Word was a god" (indefinite). He argues from Koine Greek grammar that the anarthrous predicate nominative construction indicates the nature or quality of the Word (divine nature), not indefinite identity, and that rendering it "a god" creates a contradiction with the strict monotheism of both the Old and New Testaments.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20240511_Christian Reads Isaiah 53 To A Liberal Jew and He admits THIS...!.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic reads Isaiah 53 to a Jewish interlocutor who admits the passage does not obviously describe the nation of Israel and that ancient rabbis debated its meaning. GodLogic uses this to argue that the messianic reading of Isaiah 53 was present in Jewish thought before Christianity and that Jesus fulfilled it uniquely.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20250606_When Muslims Quote Isaiah 42… DO THIS!.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic provides a response to the Muslim use of Isaiah 42 as a prophecy of Muhammad. He argues Isaiah 42:1 opens "Here is my servant, whom I uphold, my chosen one in whom I delight" — language applied to Jesus at his baptism (Matthew 12:18-21 explicitly quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 and applies it to Jesus). He shows Matthew's explicit application forecloses the Muslim interpretation.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20240405_Ali Dawah vs GodLogic FULL DISCUSSION! ｜ Is Jesus A Muslim？.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic debates Ali Dawah on whether Jesus can be considered a Muslim in the Islamic sense (one who submits to God). GodLogic argues Jesus accepted worship (Matthew 28:17, John 20:28), claimed equality with God (John 5:18), and said "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30) — none of which a Muslim prophet would do. He argues the Islamic Jesus is a reconstruction that contradicts the historical sources.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20251125_Muslim Says Only God Is "The First & The Last"… Then Sees Jesus Say It.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic shows a Muslim that Isaiah 44:6 and 48:12 identify God as "the First and the Last" — a unique divine title — and then shows Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 where Jesus applies the same title to himself. He argues this is one of the clearest New Testament claims to divine identity, where Jesus takes a title the Old Testament reserves exclusively for God.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20240814_GodLogic HUMILIATES Sheikh Uthman In LIVE Debate ｜ Is Jesus A Muslim？.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic presses the "Islamic Dilemma": Quran 10:94 instructs Muhammad to consult the People of the Book if he is in doubt — "if you are in doubt about what We have revealed to you, then ask those who have been reading the scripture before you" — implying the scriptures available in Muhammad's time were reliable. But Muslims also claim those same scriptures have been corrupted. GodLogic argues this is a self-defeating position.

source_file: GodLogic Apologetics/20231209_Young Christian and Muslim Friends Learn They Have Two Different Gods!.en.vtt
argument_summary: GodLogic walks through key differences between the Quranic Allah and the Biblical God, noting that Quran 4:171 calls Jesus "a Word from Allah and a Spirit from Him" — unique titles given to no other prophet. He argues these titles in Islamic theology are logically inconsistent with Jesus being merely a prophet, and that the Quran's own description of Jesus points toward his divine nature.

source_file: JihadWatchVideo/jihad_quran_9_5_sword_verse.en.vtt
argument_summary: JihadWatch examines Quran 9:5 — "when the sacred months have passed, kill the polytheists wherever you find them" (the Sword Verse) — cited as evidence of inherent violence in Islamic scripture. The critical perspective argues this verse abrogates earlier peaceful verses and has been used historically to justify offensive warfare. Muslim scholars counter that the verse applies to a specific historical context of war with pagan Arabs who broke treaties.

source_file: RaymondIbrahim-HW/quran_4_34_wife_beating.en.vtt
argument_summary: Raymond Ibrahim examines Quran 4:34 which instructs husbands to admonish, then abandon in bed, then "strike" (Arabic: idribuhunna) wives who show persistent disloyalty. He argues the plain reading permits physical discipline and has been implemented as such in many Islamic legal traditions. Muslim scholars dispute the translation and argue the verse refers to a light, non-harmful tap or even symbolic gesture.
